claims.claim: On 29 March 2026 at 11:40am in Klang, a 20-year-old driving a Honda City — positive for both alcohol and drugs — crossed into oncoming traffic and struck motorcyclist Amirul Hafiz Omar head-on.
claims.source: BERNAMA, The Star, Media Selangor, Sinar Daily
claims.correction: The victim's age is 33, not 34. The suspect's age is described as 'in his 20s', not specifically 20.

claims.claim: He was 34, worked two jobs to support three children, and had left home to buy a maths book for his child.
Within 24 hours, at least four MPs called for capital punishment.
claims.correction: Machang MP Wan Ahmad Fayhsal and PAS Youth chief Afnan Hamimi explicitly called for death penalty. A third (V. Papparaidu) called for maximum jail and caning. 'At least four' is supported by context.

claims.claim: The Home Affairs Minister told Parliament in November 2024 that just 149 drunk driving cases had been charged in court since January 2021.
claims.correction: Confirmed. 149 cases charged Jan 2021 to Sep 2024.

claims.claim: Transport Minister Anthony Loke reported 1,261 convictions for driving under the influence in 2025 alone.
claims.correction: The 1,261 figure is not attributed to Loke in available sources. The figure may be from BikesRepublic citing police data. The 149 figure is Section 44 only (injury/death cases); the 1,261 appears to be across Sections 44, 45, and 45A combined. These are NOT the same legal bucket.

claims.claim: Police statistics show alcohol-impaired driving caused 0.2% of fatal crashes in 2023-2024 — roughly 12-13 deaths per year out of 6,400+.
claims.source: China Press citing Transport Minister in Parliament (Oct 17, 2025)

claims.claim: Unlike Australia, where mandatory post-crash blood testing of all fatal crash drivers reveals alcohol involvement in 25-30% of cases, Malaysia tests only when officers suspect intoxication or the driver refuses a breathalyser.
claims.correction: Australia's mandatory testing is documented (Victoria since 1976, NSW updated laws) but the 25-30% figure needs specific sourcing. Malaysia's testing is discretionary per Section 45B (reasonable cause).

claims.claim: 10-15 years for fatal DUI since the 2020 amendment, BAC limit lowered from 80mg to 50mg per 100ml.
Motorcyclists account for 59% of Malaysia's 6,000+ annual road deaths — rising to 66.4% in 2025 data.
claims.source: The Star (Jan 27, 2026) — Transport Minister: 4,340 of 6,537 road deaths in 2025 = 66.4%
claims.correction: 59% is the historical average (MIROS). 66.4% is the 2025 figure per Loke.

claims.claim: Only 3% of road infrastructure has a 3-star or better safety rating for motorcycles.
claims.source: Asian Transport Observatory 2025 profile referenced but specific figure not confirmed in search

claims.claim: The Gig Workers Bill 2025 mandates SOCSO registration by platform operators.
claims.correction: Bill exists and passed Senate Sep 2025 but specific SOCSO mandate clause not confirmed in search.

claims.claim: Over 1.2 million gig workers ride Malaysia's roads daily.
claims.correction: DOSM reports exist on gig workers but specific 1.2M figure not confirmed.

claims.claim: Religious Affairs Minister Zulkifli Hasan proposed diyat as a mechanism for victim families.
Malaysia abolished mandatory death penalty in 2023.
claims.source: Public record — Abolition of Mandatory Death Penalty Act 2023, effective July 4, 2023

claims.claim: Road crashes cost ~RM25.6 billion annually (~4% of GDP).
claims.source: N/A — NST reported RM3.12 million per road death but total figure not confirmed
claims.correction: Needs MIROS or MOT primary source.

omitted_facts: Driver described as 'man in his 20s', not specifically 20.
The 149 and 1,261 figures cover DIFFERENT legal sections — 149 is Section 44 only (injury/death), 1,261 is Sections 44+45+45A combined. This is a critical category mismatch.
4,340 motorcycle deaths out of 6,537 total in 2025 (66.4%) — more current than the 59% figure.
MMA proposed a victim compensation fund and mandatory legal aid — a concrete civil society proposal not mentioned.
source_assessment: Good diversity: BERNAMA (state agency), The Star, Sinar Daily, China Press, Malay Mail, international (SCMP). Parliamentary records accessible. Weakness: some statistical claims (3% infrastructure, 1.2M gig workers, RM25.6B cost) lack primary source confirmation in available search results.
overall_assessment: Core incident facts are solid. Key statistics (149 prosecutions, 0.2% fatality share, penalty structure, motorcycle share) are verified. Critical correction needed: the 149 vs 1,261 comparison is a category mismatch (Section 44 vs Sections 44+45+45A) that undermines the governance card. The victim's age should be corrected to 33. Several broader statistics are unverified and should be hedged or sourced more carefully. Overall factual accuracy is moderate — strong on the incident, weaker on the systemic claims.
